questions.question: How is 'Menstrual Age' defined in relation to 'Embryologic Age'?
choices: Menstrual Age starts at the time of fertilization
Menstrual Age is only used after the 1st trimester
questions.detail: Menstrual age is calculated by adding 14 days to the embryologic age.

questions.question: Which term describes a pregnancy between 0 and 10 weeks of menstrual age?
questions.detail: The pregnancy is considered an embryo from 0 to 10 weeks menstrual age.

questions.question: A 'Threatened Abortion' is clinically defined as vaginal bleeding occurring with a:
questions.detail: A threatened abortion involves bleeding but the cervix remains closed.

questions.question: How is an 'Inevitable Abortion' distinguished from other early pregnancy failures?
choices: Cervical dilation is present, often with tissue hanging out
All products of conception have been expelled
It only occurs in the 2nd trimester
It is associated with a molar pregnancy
questions.detail: An inevitable abortion features cervical dilation and/or protruding tissue.

questions.question: 'Incomplete Abortion' refers to a clinical scenario where:
choices: No products are left in the uterus
Residual products of conception remain in the uterus
The fetus is alive but the sac is ruptured
The cervix is closed and bleeding has stopped
There is a twin pregnancy with one survivor
questions.detail: In an incomplete abortion, some residual products stay in the uterus.

questions.question: Which term is used when all products of conception have been expelled from the uterus?
questions.detail: A complete abortion means all products have come out.

questions.question: A 'Missed Abortion' is defined as a situation where:
choices: The mother did not realize she was pregnant
The fetus is dead but still remains inside the uterus
There is heavy bleeding with a closed cervix
Contrast was missed during an HSG
questions.detail: A missed abortion occurs when the fetus has died but remains in the uterus.

questions.question: The 'Intradecidual Sign' is an early gestational sac that can typically be seen at:
questions.detail: This early sign of pregnancy is visible around 4.5 weeks.

questions.question: To identify a true 'Intradecidual Sign,' what landmark should be seen passing by the sac?
choices: The thin echogenic line of the uterine cavity
questions.detail: The echogenic uterine cavity line should pass by the sac to confirm its location.

questions.question: The 'Double Decidual Sac Sign' is produced by visualizing which layers?
questions.detail: This sign is created by the layers of the decidua vera and decidua capsularis.

questions.question: What is the first structure typically visible within the gestational sac (GS)?
questions.detail: The yolk sac is the first structure seen inside the GS.

questions.question: According to classic teaching, a yolk sac should always be seen when the GS diameter reaches:
questions.detail: A yolk sac should be visible once the GS measures 8 mm on TVUS.

questions.question: What is the upper limit of normal for the size of a yolk sac?
questions.detail: A normal yolk sac should be smaller than 6 mm.

questions.question: A 'Yolk Sac Gone Bad' includes findings such as being too small (<3 mm), solid, calcified, or:
questions.detail: A yolk sac is suspicious if it is larger than 6 mm.

questions.question: At approximately what gestational age do the amnion and chorion typically fuse?
questions.detail: Normal amnion-chorion fusion occurs around 14–16 weeks.

questions.question: Disruption of the amnion before 10 weeks can lead to which severe condition?
questions.detail: Early disruption allows the fetus to become tangled in fibrous bands, causing amniotic band syndrome.

questions.question: What is the 'Double Bleb Sign'?
choices: The earliest visualization of the embryo (yolk and amniotic sacs with flat embryo)
questions.detail: The double bleb sign is the earliest look at the embryo between two sacs.

questions.question: Which measurement is considered the most accurate way to estimate gestational age?
questions.detail: CRL is more accurate than menstrual history for dating.

questions.question: An embryo is normally visible by ultrasound at:
questions.detail: The embryo is typically visible by 6 weeks.

questions.question: An 'Anembryonic Pregnancy' refers to a gestational sac that:
choices: Is located in the fallopian tube
Has a normal yolk sac but no heartbeat
questions.detail: This refers to a gestational sac without an embryo.

questions.question: A 'Pseudogestational Sac' is an imaging finding associated with:
questions.detail: A pseudogestational sac is seen in the uterus when there is an ectopic pregnancy.

questions.question: Which prognostic factor is most strongly associated with fetal demise in the setting of a subchorionic hemorrhage?
questions.detail: The risk of abortion increases significantly with the degree of placental detachment.

questions.question: A subchorionic hematoma is associated with a 2x increased risk of abortion if it involves more than:
questions.detail: Detachment of greater than 2/3 the circumference carries a 2x increased risk.

questions.question: According to strict criteria, a 'Pregnancy Failure' is diagnosed if the CRL is >7 mm and there is:
questions.detail: A CRL > 7 mm with no heartbeat is diagnostic of failure.

questions.question: Pregnancy failure is also diagnosed if the Mean Sac Diameter (MSD) is at least:
questions.detail: An MSD of 25 mm or more without an embryo indicates failure.

questions.question: If a scan shows a GS without a yolk sac, pregnancy failure is certain if a follow-up scan how many weeks later still shows no heartbeat?
questions.detail: No embryo with a heartbeat 2 weeks after a sac without a yolk sac is diagnostic of failure.

questions.question: If a scan shows a GS WITH a yolk sac, failure is diagnosed if no heartbeat is seen on follow-up after:
questions.detail: The threshold is 11 days if a yolk sac was previously present.

questions.question: Approximately what percentage of ectopic pregnancies occur in the fallopian tube?
questions.detail: Nearly 95% of ectopics are tubal.

questions.question: Which specific part of the fallopian tube is the most common site for an ectopic pregnancy?
questions.detail: Most occur in the ampulla.

questions.question: An 'Interstitial' ectopic pregnancy occurs where the tube passes through the uterine wall; it is high risk because:
choices: It can grow large before rupture and cause catastrophic hemorrhage
It cannot be seen on ultrasound
questions.detail: Interstitial ectopics are dangerous due to their potential for massive bleeding.

questions.question: At what serum Beta-hCG level (mIU/L) should a gestational sac typically be seen on ultrasound?
questions.detail: You should see a sac by 1500–2000 mIU/L.

questions.question: A yolk sac should generally be visible by ultrasound when the Beta-hCG reaches:
questions.detail: At 5000 mIU/L, a yolk sac should be present.

questions.question: The 'Tubal Ring Sign' is an echogenic ring surrounding an un-ruptured ectopic pregnancy and is approximately:
questions.detail: The tubal ring sign is 95% specific for ectopic pregnancy.

questions.question: A 'Heterotopic Pregnancy' is defined as:
choices: A twin pregnancy where one twin is a mole
A baby in the uterus AND a baby in an ectopic location (e.g., the tube)
A pregnancy in a unicornuate uterus
An ectopic pregnancy in the cervix
A pregnancy with two different fathers
questions.detail: This involves simultaneous intrauterine and extrauterine pregnancies.

questions.question: If the Beta-hCG is positive but the uterus is empty, the presence of adnexal findings (other than a corpus luteum) has a Positive Predictive Value (PPV) for ectopic pregnancy of:
questions.detail: Adnexal findings carry a 75–85% PPV for ectopic.

questions.question: In a patient with an empty uterus and positive hCG, the addition of a moderate volume of free fluid increases the PPV for ectopic pregnancy to:
questions.detail: Moderate free fluid increases the PPV to 97%.

questions.question: True or False: A normal doubling time for serum Beta-hCG makes an ectopic pregnancy LESS likely.
questions.detail: A normal doubling time is a reassuring sign against ectopic pregnancy.

questions.question: Biparietal Diameter (BPD) should be measured at the level of the:
questions.detail: BPD is recorded at the level of the thalamus.

questions.question: BPD measurement is taken from the outermost edge of the near skull to the:
choices: Outermost edge of the far skull
Inner table of the far skull
questions.detail: The standard is outer-to-inner table.

questions.question: Which skull shape can result in a 'false large' BPD measurement?
questions.detail: Brachycephaly can make the BPD appear falsely large.

questions.question: Unlike BPD, the 'Head Circumference' measurement is:
choices: Taken at the level of the cerebellum
questions.detail: Head circumference is a more stable measurement relative to head shape.

questions.question: Head Circumference measurement should NOT include the:
questions.detail: The skin is excluded from the HC measurement.

questions.question: Abdominal Circumference (AC) is recorded at the level of the junction of the:
choices: Umbilical vein and Left Portal vein
questions.detail: The junction of the umbilical and left portal veins is the target level.

questions.question: AC measurement should NOT include which of the following?
questions.detail: Subcutaneous soft tissues are excluded from AC.

questions.question: 'Femur Length' (FL) is the longest dimension of the femoral shaft and does NOT include the:
questions.detail: The femoral epiphysis is not included in the FL.

questions.question: In the 2nd and 3rd trimesters, gestational age estimates are referred to as a:
questions.detail: Dating in later pregnancy uses a composite of several parameters.

questions.question: Ultrasound GA estimates in the 2nd trimester (12–18 weeks) are accurate to within:
questions.detail: Accuracy is approximately 1.2 weeks in the early 2nd trimester.

questions.question: Accuracy of ultrasound GA estimates at 36–42 weeks decreases to:
questions.detail: Accuracy drops to 3.1 weeks near term.

questions.question: Estimated Fetal Weight (EFW) is typically calculated by the machine using either BPD and AC, or:
questions.detail: AC and FL are standard parameters for EFW calculation.

questions.question: For the 'Biophysical Profile,' a vertical amniotic fluid pocket is considered normal if it measures:
questions.detail: At least one pocket > 2 cm is required for points in the BPP.

questions.question: When can you first typically start to visualize the placenta on ultrasound?
questions.detail: The placenta begins as focal thickening around 8 weeks.

questions.question: At what gestational age should the placenta take on its characteristic 'disc' shape?
questions.detail: The disc shape is established by 12 weeks.

questions.question: The normal sonographic appearance of the placental parenchyma is described as:
questions.detail: The parenchyma appears granular.

questions.question: Placental 'Venous Lakes' are described as focal hypoechoic areas that are:
choices: An incidental finding of no significance
questions.detail: Venous lakes generally have no clinical significance.

questions.question: Which placental morphology consists of two near-equal sized lobes connected by a thin strip?
questions.detail: A bilobed placenta has two equal lobes.

questions.question: A 'Succenturiate Lobe' is defined as:
choices: One or more small accessory lobes
A placenta that covers the cervix
questions.detail: This refers to small accessory lobes separate from the main placental mass.

questions.question: Both bilobed and succenturiate placentas carry an increased risk for:
choices: Type 2 vasa previa and post-partum hemorrhage from retained tissue
questions.detail: These variants predispose to vasa previa and retained products.

questions.question: A 'Circumvallate Placenta' is characterized by:
choices: Rolled placental edges with a smaller chorionic plate
questions.detail: It features rolled edges with a smaller chorionic plate.

questions.question: Patients with a circumvallate placenta are at high risk for:
questions.detail: Abruption and growth restriction are key risks.

questions.question: A placenta is considered 'Too Thin' if it measures less than:
questions.detail: < 1 cm is the threshold for a thin placenta.

questions.question: Which of the following is a cause of an abnormally thin placenta?
questions.detail: Insufficiency and hypertension lead to thin placentas.

questions.question: A placenta is considered 'Too Thick' if its measurement exceeds:
questions.detail: > 4 cm defines a thick placenta.

questions.question: Which condition is associated with an abnormally THICK placenta?
questions.detail: Hydrops and DM are classic causes of thick placentas.

questions.question: 'Placental Abruption' is characteristically described as:
questions.detail: Abruption is typically painful.

questions.question: The hallmark imaging buzzword for placental abruption is:
questions.detail: Look for the disruption of the retroplacental blood vessels.

questions.question: How can you distinguish placental abruption from a myometrial contraction or fibroid on imaging?
choices: Abruption DISRUPTS the retroplacental complex; fibroids DISPLACE it
There is no way to tell
questions.detail: Disruption vs. displacement of the complex is the key discriminator.

questions.question: 'Placenta Previa' is clinically characterized by:
choices: PAINLESS vaginal bleeding in the third trimester
questions.detail: The hallmark of previa is painless bleeding.

questions.question: To avoid a 'False Positive' diagnosis of placenta previa, ultrasound should be performed with an:
questions.detail: A full bladder can compress the lower segment and mimic previa.

questions.question: A 'Low-Lying' placenta is defined as having its margin within how many cm of the internal cervical os?
questions.detail: The margin must be within 2 cm of the os.

questions.question: 'Placenta Accreta' is the most common (75%) and mildest form of creta where the villi:
choices: Attach to the myometrium without invading
questions.detail: In accreta, the villi attach directly to the myometrium.

questions.question: 'Placenta Increta' is defined as villi that:
choices: Exit the uterus into the bowel
Are only seen in molar pregnancies
questions.detail: Increta involves partial invasion into the uterine wall.

questions.question: 'Placenta Percreta' is the most severe form where villi penetrate through the:
questions.detail: Percreta goes through the uterine wall and beyond.

questions.question: What are the two primary risk factors for the development of Placenta Creta?
questions.detail: Prior C-section and previa are the leading risk factors.

questions.question: On ultrasound, a 'Placenta Creta' characteristically shows a:
questions.detail: Look for the 'moth-eaten' appearance with vascular channels.

questions.question: 'Placenta Chorioangioma' is defined as a:
choices: Hamartoma of the placenta (most common benign tumor)
questions.detail: It is the most common benign placental tumor.

questions.question: How do you distinguish a Placenta Chorioangioma from a placental hematoma?
choices: Chorioangioma has pulsating Doppler flow at the fetal heart rate
Hematomas are always near the cord
questions.detail: Pulsating flow confirms it is a chorioangioma.

questions.question: Large chorioangiomas (> 4 cm) can cause which fetal complication?
questions.detail: They can sequester platelets and cause high output heart failure.

questions.question: 'Velamentous Cord Insertion' is a variant where the cord inserts:
choices: In the center of the placenta
Into the fetal membranes outside the placental margin
questions.detail: It inserts into the membranes first.

questions.question: A 'Marginal Cord Insertion' is defined as a cord inserting within how many cm of the placental margin?
questions.detail: Insertion within 2 cm of the edge is considered marginal.

questions.question: 'Vasa Previa' refers to fetal vessels that:
choices: Cross or almost cross the internal cervical os
Are only found in the liver
questions.detail: These vessels cross the cervix, putting them at risk for rupture.

questions.question: 'Type 2 Vasa Previa' is associated with which placental variants?
questions.detail: Type 2 vessels connect to accessory or bilobed parts.

questions.question: A 'Nuchal Cord' is the term for a cord that is:
choices: Wrapped around the neck of the fetus
Too short to reach the baby
questions.detail: This is a cord around the fetal neck.

questions.question: Umbilical cord cysts occur in about 3% of pregnancies; those located CENTRALLY are typically:
questions.detail: Allantoic cysts are usually central.

questions.question: Umbilical cord cysts located PERIPHERALLY are typically:
questions.detail: Omphalomesenteric cysts are peripheral.

questions.question: If umbilical cord cysts persist into the 2nd or 3rd trimester, they are associated with:
questions.detail: Persistent cysts warrant a search for chromosomal issues.

questions.question: In fetal biometry, a 'Short Femur' (below the 5th percentile) should make you think of:
questions.detail: Short limbs are a hallmark of skeletal dysplasia.

questions.question: The 'Double Bubble' sign on antenatal ultrasound or MRI is diagnostic of:
questions.detail: This sign indicates duodenal atresia.

questions.question: 'Echogenic Bowel' is considered abnormal if its brightness is equal to:
questions.detail: Bowel should not be as bright as bone.

questions.question: The differential for echogenic bowel includes Cystic Fibrosis, Downs, and:
questions.detail: These are key associations for bright fetal bowel.

questions.question: 'Autosomal Recessive Polycystic Kidney Disease' (ARPKD) classically presents as:
choices: Massively enlarged bilateral kidneys with oligohydramnios
questions.detail: Fetal ARPKD features huge, bright kidneys.

questions.question: 'Posterior Urethral Valves' (PUV) result in which classic imaging finding?
choices: Bilateral hydro (with thick bladder wall) on fetal US or MRI
questions.detail: PUV causes high-pressure bilateral obstruction.

questions.question: 'Sacrococcygeal Teratoma' is the most common tumor of the fetus and typically causes:
choices: Mass effect on the GI system, hip dislocation, or high output cardiac failure
questions.detail: This large pelvic mass can cause severe systemic complications.

questions.question: Which skeletal measurement recorded at the same slice as BPD is affected LESS by head shape?
questions.detail: HC is more stable across different head shapes than BPD.

questions.question: 'Composite GA' is typically calculated using BPD, HC, AC, and:
questions.detail: FL is the fourth major component of composite dating.

questions.question: For fetal biometry, AC is specifically recorded at the level where what is visible?
questions.detail: The stomach bubble is typically visible at theportal/umbilical junction.

questions.question: A 'Thin Placenta' (< 1cm) is a sign of:
questions.detail: Thin placentas indicate insufficiency.

questions.question: 'Placenta Accreta' involves the villi attaching to the:
questions.detail: Villi attach directly to the muscle in accreta.

questions.question: 'Vasa Previa Type 1' occurs when vessels connect to a:
choices: Velamentous cord insertion within the main placental body
questions.detail: Type 1 is linked to velamentous insertion.

questions.question: A vertical fluid pocket > 8 cm on ultrasound is the definition of:
questions.detail: A single pocket > 8 cm defines polyhydramnios.

questions.question: 'Amniotic Band Syndrome' can cause which of the following severe complications?
questions.detail: The bands can constrict and sever fetal parts.
